# Figure description: 2_Карта_факта_M-42-IX.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/M-42-IX/2_Карта_факта_M-42-IX.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

**Карта фактического материала (Map of Factual Data / Data Coverage Map)** — this is a data-point distribution map showing the spatial locations of all survey points, boreholes, and observation stations used during the geological-geophysical survey. It is a standard Soviet-era "фактический материал" map used to document data coverage and quality control.

---

## Coverage and Subject

- **Map sheet:** М-42-IX (clearly labeled at top center)
- **Scale:** 1:200,000
- **Year:** 1984 (labeled top left: *1984г.*)
- **Area:** A portion of northern Kazakhstan, corresponding to the М-42-IX quadrangle at 1:200,000 scale
- The map covers an irregularly shaped survey area (note the non-rectangular boundary of active data coverage in the western and southern portions), suggesting the survey block did not fill the full sheet boundary
- **Institution:** Министерство геологии Казахской ССР, Центрально-Казахстанское геолого-производственное объединение (Ministry of Geology of the Kazakh SSR, Central Kazakhstan Geological-Production Association)

---

## Key Content

The map displays the spatial distribution of **hundreds of individual survey data points** across the sheet area, rendered as small circles (○) with alphanumeric labels. Key observations:

- **Point density is highest** in the western, northwestern, and southern portions of the sheet, with notably dense clustering in the southwest corner and along what appear to be survey traverses
- **The eastern half of the sheet** is largely empty of data points, indicating either no survey coverage or that this area was excluded from the current survey block — a blank region occupies the northeast and east
- **Linear chains of points** are visible, consistent with seismic or geophysical traverses (profiles), running roughly NNW–SSE and WSW–ENE directions
- **Individual points are labeled** with alphanumeric codes corresponding to borehole or observation station identifiers, including series prefixed with letters such as: **Л** (Л-30, Л-33, Л-47, Л-100, Л-102, Л-112, Л-123, Л-129, Л-133, Л-148, etc.), **А** (А-97, А-98, А-99, А-100, А-30, А-31, etc.), **Р** (Р-29, Р-30, Р-32, Р-33, Р-36, Р-37, Р-350), **С** (С-15, С-16, С-18, С-20, С-21, С-24), **Б** (Б-37, Б-38, Б-39), **О** (О-40, О-41, О-45, О-49, О-51, О-54, О-55, О-59, О-69), **Д** (Д-123, Д-130, Д-137, Д-150, Д-153), and others
- **Numbered points without letter prefixes** also appear (e.g., 359, 375, 377, 4039б, 2163, 17566, 17292, 8202, etc.), likely referring to geophysical stations or previously catalogued points
- Some points have **concentric circle symbols** (double circles ⊙), indicating special significance — possibly production boreholes, reference wells, or mineral occurrence sites
- **Dotted/dashed lines** connect sequences of points, representing survey traverses or geophysical profiles
- A **stamp** (синяя печать/blue rubber stamp) is visible in the upper right area of the map reading: *Казахская ССР / Республиканский / геологический фонд / Инв. №* [inventory number partially legible, appears to be 262/12 or similar]
- A handwritten number **(15)** in orange/red appears in the upper right corner, likely a figure number within the report

## Research Value

This map documents the **spatial distribution and identification codes of all data collection points** (boreholes, geophysical stations, and traverse stations) used in the 1983–1984 survey of sheet М-42-IX, providing an essential reference for locating specific measurements within the dataset. A geologist or AI research assistant can use it to determine **which areas have data coverage, where the survey traverses ran, and how individual point codes (e.g., Л-series, А-series boreholes) map geographically** — critical for correlating borehole logs, seismic profiles, and geophysical anomalies shown in companion figures. The notably **uneven coverage** (dense in west/south, absent in east) directly reflects the survey block boundaries and informs any interpretation of subsurface structure or mineral potential as being constrained to the western portion of the sheet.
